title: Migraine
summary: A neurological condition causing severe throbbing headache, usually one-sided, with nausea, vomiting, and sensitivity to light and sound. Debilitating but manageable with correct treatment.
steps: Act at the first warning signs. Migraine is much easier to treat at the earliest stage — waiting until pain is severe makes medication far less effective.
Take pain medication immediately at the first sign of migraine — ibuprofen 400 to 600mg or paracetamol 1000mg. Take with a small amount of food and water.
Move to a dark, quiet room immediately. Light and sound dramatically worsen migraine and prolong the attack.
Lie down with eyes closed. Sleep is one of the most effective treatments for migraine — many attacks resolve completely with sleep.
Apply a cold compress to the forehead or back of the neck.
Stay hydrated — dehydration triggers and worsens migraines. Sip water consistently even if nauseous.
If nausea or vomiting prevents taking tablets: prochlorperazine (Stemetil) dissolves under the tongue and controls nausea, allowing other medication to be taken.

Identify your triggers over time: common triggers include skipped meals, dehydration, poor sleep, stress, strong smells, bright lights, and certain foods such as aged cheese, red wine, and processed meat.
Rest for at least 1 to 2 hours after the headache resolves — the post-migraine phase causes fatigue and sensitivity even after the pain is gone.
warnings: Do not take pain medication more than 3 days per week for migraine — causes medication overuse headache which is harder to treat.
A headache described as the worst headache of your life coming on suddenly is not migraine — it is an emergency.
Do not drive or operate machinery during a migraine.
Migraine with aura (visual disturbances before the headache) combined with oral contraceptive pill use increases stroke risk — discuss with a doctor.
redFlags: Thunderclap headache — sudden maximum severity within seconds — not migraine, emergency.
Headache with fever, stiff neck, or rash.
Headache with confusion, weakness, or slurred speech — possible stroke.
Migraine lasting more than 72 hours (status migrainosus).
Significant change in usual migraine pattern.
First ever migraine in someone over 40.

helpful: Ibuprofen 600mg taken at first sign of attack — most effective OTC treatment
Prochlorperazine 5mg buccal tablet (dissolves under tongue) for nausea
Aspirin 900mg is effective for migraine in adults who are not children
avoid: Taking pain medication more than 3 days per week
Bright screens and lights during attack
askFor: Sumatriptan 50mg — prescription triptan specifically for migraine, most effective treatment
Preventive medication such as propranolol or amitriptyline if migraines are frequent
Neurologist referral for frequent or complex migraines

whenToGetHelp: Go to hospital immediately for any sudden severe headache unlike previous headaches, headache with fever and stiff neck, or headache with neurological symptoms. See a doctor if migraines are occurring more than twice per month, lasting more than 72 hours, or not responding to medication.
